perturbation.original_text: 8.1.1 the Recipient must return or procure the return to the Disclosers or, as the Disclosers may require, destroy or procure the destruction of any and all materials containing the Confidential Information together with all copies;
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1.1 the Recipient should endeavor to return or procure the return to the Disclosers or, as the Disclosers may suggest, manage or procure the management of materials containing the Confidential Information together with copies, as appropriate;
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information by replacing 'must return' with 'should endeavor to return' and 'destroy' with 'manage'. 'As the Disclosers may require' becomes 'as the Disclosers may suggest'. Adding 'as appropriate' introduces further ambiguity. This makes the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Regulations 2018 require appropriate measures to ensure the confidentiality of trade secrets. By changing the 'must return or destroy' to 'endeavor to return or manage as appropriate,' the clause introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to actively protect trade secrets upon request. Regulation 4 implies a stricter obligation than 'endeavor to manage'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 10 CFR Part 4 - NONDISCRIMINATION IN FEDERALLY ASSISTED PROGRAMS OR ACTIVITIES RECEIVING FEDERAL FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE FROM THE COMMISSION | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 10 CFR Part 4 - NONDISCRIMINATION IN FEDERALLY ASSISTED PROGRAMS OR ACTIVITIES RECEIVING FEDERAL FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE FROM THE COMMISSION CFR prev | next General Provisions (§§ 4.1 - 4.8) § 4.1 Purpose and scope. § 4.2 Subparts. § 4.3 Application of this part. § 4.4 Definitions. § 4.5 Communications and reports. § 4.6 Maintenance of records. § 4.8 Information collection requirements: OMB approval. Subpart A—Regulations Implementing Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Title IV of the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974 (§§ 4.11 - 4.93) Subpart B—Regulations Implementing Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as Amended (§§ 4.101 - 4.233) Subpart C—Regulations Implementing the Age Discrimination Act of 1975, as Amended (§§ 4.301 - 4.341) Subpart D [Reserved] Subpart E—Enforcement of Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Programs or Activities Conducted by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (§§ 4.501 - 4.571-4.999) Appendix A to Part 4—Federal Financial Assistance to Which This Part Applies 1 Authority: Atomic Energy Act of 1954, secs. 161, 223, 234, 274 ( 42 U.S.C. 2201 , 2273 , 2282 , 2021 ); Energy Reorganization Act of 1974, secs. 201, 401 ( 42 U.S.C. 5841 , 5891 ); 29 U.S.C. 794 ; 42 U.S.C. 12101 et seq.; 44 U.S.C. 3504 note. Subpart A also issued under 42 U.S.C. 2000d through d-7. Subpart B also issued under 29 U.S.C. 706 . Subpart C also issued under 42 U.S.C. 6101 through 6107 . Source: 29 FR 19277 , Dec. 31, 1964, unless otherwise noted. Editorial Note: Nomenclature changes to part 4 appear at 77 FR 39904 , July 6, 2012. CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return or destruction of confidential information ('must return or procure the return to the Disclosers...destroy or procure the destruction'). The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should endeavor to return or manage as appropriate,' making the handling of confidential information discretionary. The Law Explanation states that the Trade Secrets Regulations 2018 require appropriate measures to ensure the confidentiality of trade secrets and regulation 4 implies a stricter obligation than 'endeavor to manage'. Since the altered text weakens the obligation to safeguard trade secrets, it introduces ambiguity and falls short of the stricter compliance standards required for the protection of trade secrets, especially upon request by the disclosing party. The scraped snippets don't directly address this issue, however, the Law explanation, the Perturbation explanation and the comparison of the original and changed text lead to the conclusion that the changes made introduce an ambiguity which weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 18 Compliance with law Each party shall comply with all laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes applicable to them and shall maintain such authorisations and all other approvals, permits and authorities as are required from time to time to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 18 Compliance with law Each party shall generally comply with applicable laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes, and shall seek to maintain authorizations and approvals as needed to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'generally' before 'comply' and changing 'shall maintain' to 'shall seek to maintain' and replacing 'are required' with 'as needed,' the obligation to comply with all applicable laws and maintain necessary authorizations is weakened. This introduces ambiguity and makes compliance less stringent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the Bribery Act 2010 creates an offence for failing to prevent bribery. The original clause mandates full compliance with all laws. The modified clause introduces ambiguity by stating 'generally comply,' which can be interpreted as not requiring full compliance. This contradicts the Bribery Act's emphasis on strict compliance to prevent bribery, implying the company does not have to guarantee total compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates strict compliance with all laws, but the changed text introduces the term 'generally comply,' weakening this obligation. The LLM Law Explanation states that the Bribery Act 2010 emphasizes strict compliance to prevent bribery, implying the company must guarantee total compliance. The 'generally' qualifier contradicts this emphasis. The phrase 'shall seek to maintain authorizations and approvals as needed' weakens the obligation to maintain required authorizations. This introduces ambiguity, contradicting the intent of laws requiring specific authorizations and approvals.

perturbation.original_text: 6 Indemnity The Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosers from and against all and any losses, damages (including any consequential, direct, indirect, special, incidental or punitive damages or loss or any other form of economic loss), liability, costs (including legal fees), expenses, claims (including any settlements of claims before or after issue of proceedings), actions, proceedings, judgment sums (including sums arising from consent orders or judgments) fines and penalties, however arising out of, or in connection with, the breach of this Agreement or the unauthorised disclosure or Use of any of the Confidential Information by the Recipient or by any of its Authorised Persons.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 Indemnity The Recipient may indemnify the Disclosers for reasonable losses and damages, subject to their discretion, related to a significant breach of this Agreement where it is directly caused by the Recipient's gross negligence. This will cover demonstrable direct costs, legal fees, and other verifiable expenses, as deemed appropriate by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates full indemnification for all losses. The modified clause changes 'shall indemnify' to 'may indemnify,' making it discretionary. It also limits the indemnity to 'reasonable losses and damages', a 'significant breach', and 'gross negligence'. The phrases 'subject to their discretion', 'demonstrable direct costs...as deemed appropriate by the Recipient' introduce further ambiguity and shift control to the Recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 232 of the Companies Act 2006 states that any provision that exempts a director (or equivalent) from liability for negligence, default, breach of duty, or breach of trust is void. While this clause doesn't directly relate to director liability, it sets a precedent where indemnity is discretionary. Suppose a director breaches their duty and causes damage. In that case, the Recipient's discretionary ability to indemnify contradicts the spirit of the Companies Act, which expects more than simply 'reasonableness' in the event of clear negligence and breach.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates indemnification ('shall indemnify'), while the modified version makes it discretionary ('may indemnify'). The Perturbation Explanation confirms this shift, highlighting the increased ambiguity and control given to the Recipient. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly usable, the Law Explanation raises concerns about compliance with the spirit of the Companies Act 2006, which expects more than 'reasonableness' in cases of negligence. The change undermines the original obligation, as the recipient now has the discretion to indemnify based on their own criteria.
